system_message:
# Scripture Study — System Context

You are a scripture study assistant collaborating with a human who brings faith in
Jesus Christ and the Restoration. Your role is to facilitate deep, honest scripture
study — producing not just knowledge but personal transformation.

## Core Values

- **Depth over breadth.** Take time to really explore. Surface patterns that casual
  reading misses. Trace words to Hebrew/Greek. Compare across all five standard works.
  Follow the footnotes.
- **Honest exploration.** Engage thoughtfully with hard questions rather than retreating
  to disclaimers. Uncertainty can be acknowledged warmly.
- **Faith as framework.** Work within the framework of faith in Jesus Christ and the
  Restoration. Offer scholarly insight AND spiritual application.

## Constraints

- Every direct quote must be accurate. Do not fabricate or approximate quotations.
- Link claims to specific scriptures. Chapter and verse, not vague references.
- When you don't know something, say so. A faithful "I'm not sure" is better than
  a confident fabrication.

## What Good Output Looks Like

- Cross-references that surface unexpected connections between standard works
- Webster 1828 word analysis that illuminates Restoration-era vocabulary
- Teaching patterns extracted from conference talks
- Doctrinal synthesis that traces themes across multiple sources
- Specific, actionable insights — not generic spiritual platitudes

## Standard Works

The five standard works are: Old Testament, New Testament, Book of Mormon,
Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price. Conference talks from
General Conference (1971–present) are also available.
response:
## Alma 32 – A Teaching‑Pattern Dissection  
*(Prepared for a Sunday‑School setting)*  

Below is a line‑by‑line breakdown of the rhetorical architecture Alma employs in **Alma 32**.  Each element is tagged with the question you asked, and the final “steal‑it” column gives you a concrete tool you can copy into your own lessons.

---

### 1. Opening Hook – Capturing Attention  

| Technique | How Alma does it | Why it works for a Sunday‑School audience |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Concrete visual cue + “it came to pass”** | Alma opens with *“And it came to pass that they did go forth, and began to preach the word of God unto the people, entering into their synagogues, and into their houses; yea, and even they did preach the word in their streets.”* (v. 1) | The phrase *“it came to pass”* signals a narrative shift; the mention of **entering synagogues and streets** instantly paints a scene that listeners can picture.  It also creates a sense of urgency (“they went forth”) that pulls the audience into the story before any doctrine is introduced. |
| **Identify the “poor” as the audience** | Verse 2‑3 describe the **poor, cast out, despised, and lowly in heart** (v. 4‑5). | By naming the **socially marginal** group, Alma creates a “you‑are‑there” moment for anyone who has ever felt excluded or humbled.  The hook is not abstract; it is *“the poor who have been driven from the synagogue.”*  This immediately raises the question: *What will happen to us?* |
| **Rhetorical question** | The foremost among them asks, *“Behold, what shall these my brethren do?”* (v. 5). | The question is **genuine**—Alma has not yet answered.  It forces every listener to pause, consider the dilemma, and prepares them for the solution that follows. |

> **Takeaway:** Start with a vivid scene that names a specific group and ends with a question that leaves a “gap” the rest of the talk will fill.

---

### 2. Story Structure – What Stories Are Told & How They Connect to Doctrine  

| Story Element | Scripture Reference | Connection to Doctrine |
|---------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **The “poor” who are cast out** | v. 2‑5; 4‑5 describe the **poor, despised, and cast out** | Sets up the **condition of humility** that makes spiritual learning possible.  Alma later links this condition to the **necessity of being humbled** (v. 12‑13). |
| **Alma’s personal response** | v. 6‑7: *“he turned him about, his face immediately towards him… and cried unto those whom he beheld, who were truly penitent.”* | Shows **personal compassion** and a **shift of focus** from the crowd to the individual who is ready to hear.  This models the principle that **the Lord directs attention to the humble heart**. |
| **The seed metaphor** | v. 28‑42 (the seed‑to‑tree illustration) | This is the **central doctrinal illustration**—the **word of God** is a seed that must be planted, nourished, and allowed to grow.  It ties **humility** (the ground) to **faith** (the seed) to **obedience** (nourishing). |
| **Future promise of the fruit** | v. 42‑43: *“ye shall reap the rewards… which is most precious… and pure above all that is pure.”* | Completes the **cause‑effect chain**: humility → planting the seed → nourishing → fruit (eternal life).  The promise gives a **forward‑looking, hopeful climax**. |

> **Takeaway:** Structure your lesson around a **progressive narrative** (problem → divine response → illustration → promise).  Each step should naturally lead to the next doctrinal point.

---

### 3. Question Usage – Rhetorical or Genuine?  

| Question | Text | Type | Function |
|----------|------|------|----------|
| **“Behold, what shall these my brethren do?”** | v. 5 | **Genuine** (the asker truly seeks an answer) | Engages the audience, creates a problem‑solving mindset. |
| **“Behold I say unto you, do ye suppose that ye cannot worship God save it be in your synagogues only?”** | v. 10 | **Rhetorical** (the answer is obvious; the question forces self‑examination) | Highlights **false limitations** and invites the listener to reconsider assumptions. |
| **“I ask, is this faith?”** | v. 18 | **Rhetorical** | Contrasts **knowledge** with **faith**, driving home the definition of faith as *hope in things not seen* (v. 21). |
| **“Now, as I said concerning faith—faith is not to have a perfect knowledge of things; therefore if ye have faith ye hope for things which are not seen, which are true.”** | v. 21‑22 | **Teaching statement with embedded question** | Clarifies the **nature of faith**; the question *“Is this faith?”* is answered by a definition that expands the concept beyond mere belief. |
| **“Now, how much more cursed is he that knoweth the will of God and doeth it not…?”** | v. 19 | **Rhetorical** | Elevates the stakes; the question is a **moral challenge** that pushes listeners toward action. |

> **Takeaway:** Mix **genuine questions** (to draw out personal reflection) with **rhetorical questions** (to expose error or reinforce a point).  Use them at strategic moments—after a story, before a key doctrine, or at the climax of an argument.

---

### 4. Scripture Integration – Proof‑Text vs. Narrative  

| Integration Mode | Example | Effect |
|------------------|---------|--------|
| **Direct quotation for definition** | Alma quotes **Mosiah 23:14** (not shown here) to define *“faith is a hope in that which is not seen which is true.”* (v. 21) | Provides **authoritative grounding**; the audience sees the doctrine is scripturally anchored. |
| **Scripture as narrative element** | Alma recounts **the poor being cast out**, **the humbling**, **the planting of the seed**—all **story events** rather than isolated proof‑texts. | The scriptures become **living illustrations**, not merely citations.  This makes the doctrine **relatable** and **memorable**. |
| **Cross‑referencing** | Alma links **Alma 33:1‑2** (the seed) and **Alma 34:31‑34** (the tree) to earlier teachings. | Shows **scriptural continuity**; the audience sees a pattern that reinforces the principle across generations. |
| **Use of footnotes** | Footnotes (e.g., “poor” → Luke 6:20) give **scriptural cross‑reference** that supports the point. | Adds **depth** for those who wish to dig deeper, but does not interrupt the flow for the average listener. |

> **Takeaway:** **Weave scriptures into the story** rather than stopping to proof‑text.  When you need a definition or doctrinal anchor, quote it briefly and then **continue the narrative**.

---

### 5. Emotional Arc – Highs and Lows  

| Point in the Chapter | Emotional Tone | Why It Matters |
|----------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Opening (v. 1‑3)** – “they went forth… entered synagogues… were cast out” | **Low** – feelings of **despair, exclusion** | Sets a **relatable low point** that the audience can empathize with. |
| **Alma’s joy (v. 6)** – “he beheld with great joy” | **High** – **hope, optimism** | Shows that **humility opens the door to divine joy**; a turning point that lifts spirits. |
| **The seed metaphor (v. 28‑31)** – “it beginneth to enlarge my soul” | **Mid‑high** – **curiosity, wonder** | Engages the mind; the **process of growth** creates a sense of possibility. |
| **Promise of fruit (v. 42‑43)** – “most precious… shall be filled” | **Peak high** – **joyful anticipation** | The **culmination** of the arc; the audience feels the **reward** of obedience. |
| **Final exhortation (v. 43)** – “ye shall reap the rewards of your faith” | **Sustained high** – **assurance, confidence** | Leaves the listener with a **positive, forward‑looking feeling** that encourages obedience. |

> **Takeaway:** Structure your talk so the **emotional trajectory moves from low → high → higher**.  Use **low points** (problems, trials) to set up a **turning point** (a question, a revelation), then **climax** with a promise of blessing.

---

### 6. Call to Action – The Specific Invitation  

> **Alma 32:27‑28** – *“Awake and arouse your faculties… exercise a particle of faith… give place for a portion of my words… let this desire work in you, even until ye believe… let this desire work in you, even until ye believe in a manner that ye can give place for a portion of my words.”*  

**What Alma asks the listener to do:**  

1. **Awake and arouse your faculties** – be mentally and spiritually alert.  
2. **Exercise a particle of faith** – start small, even a “desire to believe.”  
3. **Give place to the word** – allow the seed to be planted in your heart.  
4. **Nourish the tree** – continue to feed the word with diligence, patience, and faith.  

**Result promised:** *“ye shall reap the rewards of your faith, and your diligence, and patience, and long‑suffering, waiting for the tree to bring forth fruit” (v. 43).*

> **Takeaway:** End with a **concrete, actionable step** that is *small enough to be doable now* (e.g., “plant a seed of faith today by reading one verse and praying for understanding”) and **pair it with a vivid promise** of future blessing.

---

### 7. One Technique to **Steal**  

#### **The “Seed‑to‑Tree” Illustration + “Particle of Faith” Invitation**  

Alma’s most powerful teaching tool is **the simple, visual metaphor of a seed that must be planted, nurtured, and eventually bears fruit**.  He then **explicitly tells the listener what the first step is** (“exercise a particle of faith”).  

**Why it works for Sunday School:**  

* **Universal imagery:** Everyone knows what a seed does; it is a natural, non‑technical way to talk about spiritual growth.  
* **Action‑oriented:** The metaphor is not abstract; it comes with a **clear, doable instruction** (“give place to the word”).  
* **Memorable:** Listeners can picture a seed growing into a tree and instantly recall the steps of humility → faith → nourishment → fruit.  

**How to replicate it:**  

1. **Select a simple, everyday image** that matches a doctrinal principle (e.g., a seed, a seed‑pod, a tree, a fire).  
2. **Map each part of the image to a spiritual principle** (seed → word of God; soil → humble heart; water → prayer; sunlight → the Spirit).  
3. **State a tiny, immediate action** that corresponds to planting the seed (e.g., “Read one verse and ask the Spirit to confirm its truth”).  
4. **Promise a future outcome** (e.g., “As you keep nourishing it, you will feel peace, clarity, and eventually receive revelation”).  

> **Bottom line:** *Use a concrete, visual metaphor that includes a tiny first step and a hopeful outcome.*  This combination captures attention, makes doctrine relatable, and motivates application—all in one package.

---

## Quick “Lesson‑Plan” Sketch (5‑minute Sunday‑School Use)

| Time | Segment | Script/Technique | Goal |
|------|---------|------------------|------|
| 0:00‑0:30 | **Hook** | “Imagine you’ve been kicked out of your church because your clothes are too simple. You feel poor, ashamed, and wonder, ‘What can we do?’” | Create a vivid, relatable problem. |
| 0:30‑1:30 | **Story** | Briefly recount Alma 32:5‑6 (the poor ask, Alma turns his face with joy). | Show that God notices the humble. |
| 1:30‑2:30 | **Illustration** | “Alma says the word of God is like a seed. If you give it a little place in your heart, it will start to grow.” | Introduce metaphor; ask a rhetorical question: “Do you think a tiny seed can become a big tree?” |
| 2:30‑3:30 | **Particle of Faith** | “All you need to start is a *desire* to believe. That’s the particle of faith.” | Give the concrete first step. |
| 3:30‑4:30 | **Promise** | “If you keep nourishing it—reading, praying, obeying—you’ll reap the fruit of eternal life.” | Close with hopeful outcome. |
| 4:30‑5:00 | **Application** | “This week, find one verse, read it, and ask the Spirit to confirm its truth. Write down what you feel.” | Provide a specific, actionable task. |

---

### Final Thought  

Alma 32 is a masterclass in **story‑driven doctrine**.  He moves his audience from a **low point of humiliation** to a **high point of promised blessing** by using a **simple visual metaphor**, **purposeful questions**, and **clear invitations**.  Emulate his pattern: **set the scene, pose a genuine question, illustrate with a vivid image, give a tiny actionable step, and end with a hopeful promise**.  When you do, your lessons will not only inform the mind but also **transform the heart**.  

May the Spirit guide your preparation, and may the “seed” you plant today grow into a tree of righteous testimony. 🙏
user_message: Analyze the teaching patterns and rhetorical techniques in the following content. I'm preparing to teach in a Sunday School setting and want to learn from how this speaker communicates. Identify: 1. **Opening hook** — how do they capture attention? 2. **Story structure** — what stories are told, and how do they connect to doctrine? 3. **Question usage** — what questions do they ask, and are they rhetorical or genuine? 4. **Scripture integration** — how do they weave scriptures into the flow (proof-text vs. narrative)? 5. **Emotional arc** — where are the high and low points? 6. **Call to action** — what specific invitation do they extend? 7. **One technique I should steal** — the single most effective thing this speaker does --- # Alma 32 🎧 [Listen to Audio](https://assets.churchofjesuschrist.org/da5e0db1386611edad32eeeeac1e5cb97c263581-64k-en.mp3) Chapter 32 Alma teaches the poor whose afflictions had humbled them—Faith is a hope in that which is not seen which is true—Alma testifies that angels minister to men, women, and children—Alma compares the word unto a seed—It must be planted and nourished—Then it grows into a tree from which the fruit of eternal life is picked. About 74 B.C. **1.** And it came to pass that they did go forth, and began to preach the word of God unto the people, entering into their synagogues, and into their houses; yea, and even they did preach the word in their streets. **2.** And it came to pass that after much labor among them, they began to have success among the poor[2a](#fn-2a) class of people; for behold, they were cast out of the synagogues because of the coarseness of their apparel— **3.** Therefore they were not permitted to enter into their synagogues to worship God, being esteemed as filthiness; therefore they were poor; yea, they were esteemed by their brethren as dross[3a](#fn-3a); therefore they were poor[3b](#fn-3b) as to things of the world; and also they were poor in heart. **4.** Now, as Alma was teaching and speaking unto the people upon the hill Onidah[4a](#fn-4a), there came a great multitude[4b](#fn-4b) unto him, who were those of whom we have been speaking, of whom were poor in heart[4c](#fn-4c), because of their poverty as to the things of the world. **5.** And they came unto Alma; and the one who was the foremost among them said unto him: Behold, what[5a](#fn-5a) shall these my brethren do, for they are despised[5b](#fn-5b) of all men because of their poverty, yea, and more especially by our priests; for they have cast[5c](#fn-5c) us out of our synagogues which we have labored abundantly to build with our own hands; and they have cast us out because of our exceeding poverty; and we have no[5d](#fn-5d) place to worship our God; and behold, what[5e](#fn-5e) shall we do? **6.** And now when Alma heard this, he turned him about, his face immediately towards him, and he beheld with great joy; for he beheld that their afflictions[6a](#fn-6a) had truly humbled[6b](#fn-6b) them, and that they were in a preparation[6c](#fn-6c) to hear the word. **7.** Therefore he did say no more to the other multitude; but he stretched forth his hand, and cried unto those whom he beheld, who were truly penitent, and said unto them: **8.** I behold that ye are lowly[8a](#fn-8a) in heart; and if so, blessed are ye. **9.** Behold thy brother hath said, What shall we do?—for we are cast out of our synagogues, that we cannot worship our God. **10.** Behold I say unto you, do ye suppose that ye cannot worship[10a](#fn-10a) God save it be in your synagogues only? **11.** And moreover, I would ask, do ye suppose that ye must not worship God only once[11a](#fn-11a) in a week? **12.** I say unto you, it is well that ye are cast out of your synagogues, that ye may be humble, and that ye may learn wisdom[12a](#fn-12a); for it is necessary that ye should learn wisdom; for it is because that ye are cast out, that ye are despised of your brethren because of your exceeding poverty[12b](#fn-12b), that ye are brought to a lowliness of heart; for ye are necessarily brought to be humble. **13.** And now, because ye are compelled to be humble blessed are ye; for a man sometimes, if he is compelled to be humble, seeketh repentance[13a](#fn-13a); and now surely, whosoever repenteth shall find mercy; and he that findeth mercy and endureth[13b](#fn-13b) to the end the same shall be saved. **14.** And now, as I said unto you, that because ye were compelled to be humble[14a](#fn-14a) ye were blessed, do ye not suppose that they are more blessed who truly humble themselves because of the word? **15.** Yea, he that truly humbleth himself, and repenteth of his sins, and endureth to the end, the same shall be blessed—yea, much more blessed than they who are compelled to be humble because of their exceeding poverty. **16.** Therefore, blessed are they who humble[16a](#fn-16a) themselves without being compelled[16b](#fn-16b) to be humble; or rather, in other words, blessed is he that believeth in the word of God, and is baptized without stubbornness[16c](#fn-16c) of heart, yea, without being brought to know the word, or even compelled to know, before they will believe. **17.** Yea, there are many who do say: If thou wilt show unto us a sign[17a](#fn-17a) from heaven, then we shall know of a surety; then we shall believe. **18.** Now I ask, is this faith? Behold, I say unto you, Nay; for if a man knoweth a thing he hath no cause to believe[18a](#fn-18a), for he knoweth it. **19.** And now, how much more[19a](#fn-19a) cursed[19b](#fn-19b) is he that knoweth[19c](#fn-19c) the will[19d](#fn-19d) of God and doeth it not, than he that only believeth, or only hath cause to believe, and falleth into transgression[19e](#fn-19e)? **20.** Now of this thing ye must judge. Behold, I say unto you, that it is on the one hand even as it is on the other; and it shall be unto every man according to his work. **21.** And now as I said concerning faith—faith[21a](#fn-21a) is not to have a perfect knowledge of things; therefore if ye have faith ye hope[21b](#fn-21b) for things which are not[21c](#fn-21c) seen, which are true. **22.** And now, behold, I say unto you, and I would that ye should remember, that God is merciful[22a](#fn-22a) unto all who believe on his name; therefore he desireth, in the first place, that ye should believe, yea, even on his word. **23.** And now, he imparteth his word by angels unto men, yea, not[23a](#fn-23a) only men but women also. Now this is not all; little children[23b](#fn-23b) do have words given unto them many times, which confound[23c](#fn-23c) the wise and the learned. **24.** And now, my beloved brethren, as ye have desired to know of me what ye shall do because ye are afflicted and cast out—now I do not desire that ye should suppose that I mean to judge you only according to that which is true— **25.** For I do not mean that ye all of you have been compelled to humble yourselves; for I verily believe that there are some among you who would humble[25a](#fn-25a) themselves, let them be in whatsoever circumstances they might. **26.** Now, as I said concerning faith—that it was not a perfect knowledge—even so it is with my words. Ye cannot know of their surety at first, unto perfection, any more than faith is a perfect knowledge. **27.** But behold, if ye will awake and arouse your faculties, even to an experiment upon my words, and exercise a particle of faith, yea, even if ye can no more than desire[27a](#fn-27a) to believe, let this desire work in you, even until ye believe in a manner that ye can give place for a portion of my words. **28.** Now, we will compare the word unto a seed[28a](#fn-28a). Now, if ye give place, that a seed[28b](#fn-28b) may be planted in your heart[28c](#fn-28c), behold, if it be a true seed, or a good seed, if ye do not cast it out by your unbelief[28d](#fn-28d), that ye will resist the Spirit of the Lord, behold, it will begin to swell within your breasts; and when you feel these swelling motions, ye will begin to say within yourselves—It must needs be that this is a good seed, or that the word is good, for it beginneth to enlarge my soul; yea, it beginneth to enlighten[28e](#fn-28e) my understanding[28f](#fn-28f), yea, it beginneth to be delicious to me. **29.** Now behold, would not this increase your faith? I say unto you, Yea; nevertheless it hath not grown up to a perfect knowledge. **30.** But behold, as the seed swelleth, and sprouteth, and beginneth to grow, then you must needs say that the seed is good; for behold it swelleth, and sprouteth, and beginneth to grow. And now, behold, will not this strengthen your faith? Yea, it will strengthen your faith: for ye will say I know that this is a good seed; for behold it sprouteth and beginneth to grow. **31.** And now, behold, are ye sure that this is a good seed? I say unto you, Yea; for every seed bringeth forth unto its own likeness[31a](#fn-31a). **32.** Therefore, if a seed groweth it is good, but if it groweth not, behold it is not good, therefore it is cast away. **33.** And now, behold, because ye have tried the experiment, and planted the seed, and it swelleth and sprouteth, and beginneth to grow, ye must needs know that the seed is good. **34.** And now, behold, is your knowledge[34a](#fn-34a) perfect[34b](#fn-34b)? Yea, your knowledge is perfect in that thing, and your faith[34c](#fn-34c) is dormant; and this because you know, for ye know that the word hath swelled your souls, and ye also know that it hath sprouted up, that your understanding doth begin to be enlightened, and your mind[34d](#fn-34d) doth begin to expand. **35.** O then, is not this real? I say unto you, Yea, because it is light[35a](#fn-35a); and whatsoever is light, is good[35b](#fn-35b), because it is discernible, therefore ye must know that it is good; and now behold, after ye have tasted this light is your knowledge perfect? **36.** Behold I say unto you, Nay; neither must ye lay aside your faith, for ye have only exercised your faith to plant the seed that ye might try the experiment to know if the seed was good. **37.** And behold, as the tree beginneth to grow, ye will say: Let us nourish it with great care, that it may get root, that it may grow up, and bring forth fruit unto us. And now behold, if ye nourish it with much care it will get root, and grow up, and bring forth fruit. **38.** But if ye neglect[38a](#fn-38a) the tree, and take no thought for its nourishment, behold it will not get any root; and when the heat of the sun cometh and scorcheth it, because it hath no root it withers away, and ye pluck it up and cast it out. **39.** Now, this is not because the seed was not good, neither is it because the fruit thereof would not be desirable; but it is because your ground[39a](#fn-39a) is barren[39b](#fn-39b), and ye will not nourish the tree, therefore ye cannot have the fruit thereof. **40.** And thus, if ye will not nourish the word, looking forward with an eye of faith to the fruit thereof, ye can never pluck of the fruit of the tree of life[40a](#fn-40a). **41.** But if ye will nourish the word, yea, nourish the tree as it beginneth to grow, by your faith with great diligence, and with patience[41a](#fn-41a), looking forward to the fruit thereof, it shall take root; and behold it shall be a tree springing up[41b](#fn-41b) unto everlasting life. **42.** And because of your diligence[42a](#fn-42a) and your faith and your patience with the word in nourishing it, that it may take root in you, behold, by and by ye shall pluck the fruit[42b](#fn-42b) thereof, which is most precious, which is sweet above all that is sweet, and which is white above all that is white, yea, and pure above all that is pure; and ye shall feast upon this fruit even until ye are filled, that ye hunger not, neither shall ye thirst. **43.** Then, my brethren, ye shall reap[43a](#fn-43a) the rewards[43b](#fn-43b) of your faith, and your diligence, and patience, and long-suffering, waiting for the tree to bring forth fruit[43c](#fn-43c) unto you. --- ## Footnotes **2a. poor** — [Luke 6:20](../../nt/luke/6.md); [7:22](../../nt/luke/7.md). [TG Poor](../../tg/poor.md). **3a. dross** — [Luke 18:9](../../nt/luke/18.md). **3b. poor** — [Alma 34:40](34.md). **4a. Onidah** — [Alma 47:5](47.md). **4b. multitude** — [TG Assembly for Worship](../../tg/assembly-for-worship.md). **4c. poor in heart** — [TG Poor in Spirit](../../tg/poor-in-spirit.md). **5a. what** — [Prov. 18:23](../../ot/prov/18.md). **5b. despised** — [TG Oppression](../../tg/oppression.md). **5c. cast** — [Alma 33:10](33.md). **5d. no** — [Alma 33:2](33.md). **5e. what** — [Acts 2:37 (37–38)](../../nt/acts/2.md); [Alma 34:3](34.md). **6a. afflictions** — [TG Adversity](../../tg/adversity.md). **6b. humbled** — [TG Humility](../../tg/humility.md); [Teachable](../../tg/teachable.md). **6c. preparation** — [Prov. 16:1](../../ot/prov/16.md); [Alma 16:16 (16–17)](16.md); [D&C 101:8](../../dc-testament/dc/101.md). **8a. lowly** — [Matt. 5:5 (3–5)](../../nt/matt/5.md). **10a. cannot worship** — [TG Worship](../../tg/worship.md). **11a. once** — [Mosiah 18:25](../mosiah/18.md). **12a. wisdom** — [Eccl. 4:13](../../ot/eccl/4.md). **12b. poverty** — [Prov. 16:8](../../ot/prov/16.md); [28:11](../../ot/prov/28.md). **13a. repentance** — [TG Objectives](../../tg/objectives.md). **13b. endureth** — [Alma 38:2](38.md); [3 Ne. 15:9](../3-ne/15.md); [27:6 (6–17)](../3-ne/27.md).
